coredata.dc:title: Pd-NCQD composite confined in SBA-15 as highly active catalyst for aqueous phase catalytic hydrodechlorination of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hui
Long, Li
Xu, Zhaoyi
Zheng, Shourong
coredata.dc:description: A composite consisting of Pd particles and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) confined in mesoporous SBA-15 (Pd-NC@SBA-15) was prepared. For comparison, Pd catalysts supported on SBA-15 (im-Pd/SBA-15), NCQDs modified SBA-15 (im-Pd/NC-SBA-15) and N-doped carbon nanotubes (Pd/NCNTs) were prepared using the impregnation method. The catalysts were characterized and the aqueous phase catalytic hydrodechlorination (HDC) of 2,4- dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) was investigated. Characterization results showed that Pd-NC@SBA-15 had a higher Pd dispersion and more homogeneous Pd particle distribution than other catalysts. Additionally, the presence of NCQDs led to increased content of positively charged Pd in Pd-NC@SBA-15. As a result, Pd-NC@SBA-15 exhibited a much higher catalytic activity. Furthermore, the turnover frequency value of the Pd-NC@SBA-15 catalyst increased with Pdn+ content and remained identical at Pdn+/Pd0 ratio above 1.20. The HDC of 2,4-D on Pd-NC@SBA-15 proceeded via both stepwise and concerted pathways and the stepwise dechlorination pathway was predominant. The present study highlights that substantial enhancement of the catalytic activity can be achieved by rational NCQDs modification of Pd based catalysts for the aqueous phase catalytic removal of pollutants.

head.ce:title: Pd-NCQD composite confined in SBA-15 as highly active catalyst for aqueous phase catalytic hydrodechlorination of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A composite consisting of Pd particles and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) confined in mesoporous SBA-15 (Pd-NC@SBA-15) was prepared. For comparison, Pd catalysts supported on SBA-15 (im-Pd/SBA-15), NCQDs modified SBA-15 (im-Pd/NC-SBA-15) and N-doped carbon nanotubes (Pd/NCNTs) were prepared using the impregnation method. The catalysts were characterized and the aqueous phase catalytic hydrodechlorination (HDC) of 2,4- dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) was investigated. Characterization results showed that Pd-NC@SBA-15 had a higher Pd dispersion and more homogeneous Pd particle distribution than other catalysts. Additionally, the presence of NCQDs led to increased content of positively charged Pd in Pd-NC@SBA-15. As a result, Pd-NC@SBA-15 exhibited a much higher catalytic activity. Furthermore, the turnover frequency value of the Pd-NC@SBA-15 catalyst increased with Pdn+ content and remained identical at Pdn+/Pd0 ratio above 1.20. The HDC of 2,4-D on Pd-NC@SBA-15 proceeded via both stepwise and concerted pathways and the stepwise dechlorination pathway was predominant. The present study highlights that substantial enhancement of the catalytic activity can be achieved by rational NCQDs modification of Pd based catalysts for the aqueous phase catalytic removal of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: As a well-known herbicide in modern agriculture, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) is preferentially used in most developing countries due to its low cost and good performance [1,2]. Because of its high solubility and low adsorption affinity to soils, 2,4-D has been frequently identified as a major contaminant in both surface water and groundwater at high concentrations [3]. Numerous methods, such as physical, chemical or biological treatment, are developed to eliminate 2,4-D pollution. Among these treatment methods, aqueous phase catalytic hydrodechlorination (HDC) produces less toxic intermediates and has been recognized as a green and effective approach to substantially reduce the toxicity of the pollutants in combination of recovery of valuable chemicals [4,5]. Noble metal catalysts supported on carbonaceous materials were widely used in HDC processes due to their high stability against acid and base under liquid phase reaction conditions [6]. However, noble metals on the surface of carbonaceous materials were prone to aggregation and loss during catalyst preparation and reaction processes due to the weak metal-support interaction, giving rise to low catalytic activity and stability [7,8].

Heteroatom doping has been commonly applied to modify the structural properties of carbonaceous supports in order to modulate the catalytic performance of supported noble metal catalysts. For example, N-doping of carbonaceous materials could result in three main nitrogen-containing configurations, e.g., pyridinic, pyrrolic and quaternary nitrogen [9,10], which acted as anchoring sites to enhance metal-support interactions and improve metal dispersions [11,12]. Additionally, pyridinic N species were capable of interacting with Pd via covalent bonding, which influenced the electronic structure of Pd particles and their catalytic performance in oxygen reduction and hydrodehalogenation reactions [10,13]. In parallel, P-, S- and B-doping had been also approved to be effective to modulate the electronic structure of carbonaceous materials and thus impact the catalytic performance [14,15]. Notably, effective heteroatom doping into carbon skeleton was usually achieved at high temperature, normally resulting in low heteroatom doping level. Additionally, in noble metal catalysts metal particles randomly deposited on support surface, leading to a low bonding effect between noble metal particles and N-containing functional groups and thus low functional group utilization efficiency.

Recently, N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) have attracted considerable attention because of their unique chemical and physical properties [16,17]. Furthermore, N content and N species in NCQDs can be precisely tuned by varying preparation methods and the precursors of C or N [18,19]. For example, Dai et al [20] prepared NCQDs with N/C atomic ratios in the range of 5–15% and favorable formation of pyridinic N. Gu et al [21] concluded that the N/C ratio of NCQDs could be even higher the maximum level of 1.74. Furthermore, NCQDs can be modified by amino-groups [22], which were able to combine with Pd precursor and lead to site-specific deposition of Pd particles on NCQDs. Notably, NCQDs are nanosized with particle size normally smaller than 10 nm. Similar to other nanosized particles, NCQDs are prone to aggregating at high concentrations [23]. Confining nanoparticles in the pores of porous materials is an effective method to avoid nanoparticle aggregation and thus achieve high particle dispersion [24,25]. Ordered mesoporous SiO2 is an attractive support to host nanosized materials owing to its ordered structure, large surface area and abundant pores. Hence, we conceived that the formation of Pd-NCQD composites confined in the mesopores of SBA-15 endowed the catalyst unique catalytic properties in the 2,4-D HDC. However, similar catalysts have not been explored and reported.

The major goal of this study was to synthesize Pd-NCQD composite confined in SBA-15 pores and investigate their catalytic performance in the aqueous phase catalytic HDC of 2,4-D. Other Pd catalysts supported on N-doped carbon nanotubes (CNTs), SBA-15 and NCQDs-modified SBA-15 were also prepared by the impregnation method for comparison. In combination with characterization results of the catalysts, the dechlorination of 2,4-D in aqueous solution was systematically investigated. The results reflected that the Pd-NCQD composites exhibited superior catalytic activity for dechlorination of 2,4-D.

N-doped carbon quantum dots were prepared by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as the precursors [26,27]. Typically, 2.1 g of citric acid and 1.8 g of urea were dissolved in 50 ml of deionized water, and the mixture was treated in a 100 ml autoclave at 160 °C for 5 h. Finally, transparent brown NCQD solution was obtained and preserved in dark for further use.

Mesoporous SBA-15 support was obtained from XFNANO (Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., China) without further treatment. Pd-NCQD composite confined in SBA-15 was synthesized by the incipient wetness impregnation. Briefly, 4.5 ml of solution consisting of a certain amount of H2PdCl4 and 1–4 ml of NCQDs was stirred for 10 min under ambient conditions. Then 3.0 g of SBA-15 was impregnated in the solution with ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, which was then stirred for another 2 h to facilitate the loading of NCQDs and Pd inside SBA-15 pores. The slurry was washed with alcohol, then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Analysis of the filtrate from alcohol washing using inductive coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) showed the absence of Pd2+, suggesting that Pd precursor effectively bonded with NCQDs. The catalysts were reduced at 300 °C for 2 h in a H2 atmosphere (30 ml min−1). The obtained catalyst was designated as Pd(x)-NC(y)@SBA-15, in which x referred to the Pd loading amount (wt.%) measured by ICP-OES and y referred to the volume of NCQD solution added.

To compare catalyst activity, Pd catalysts supported on SBA-15, NCQDs modified SBA-15 (NC-SBA-15) and N-doped CNTs (NCNTs) were synthesized by the impregnation method. NC-SBA-15 support was synthesized through the same menu having 2 ml of NCQDs solution without H2PdCl4 solution. The supports were heated at 300 °C for 2 h in a 30 ml min−1 N2 stream. NCNTs support was prepared by impregnating 1.0 g CNTs with 0.5 g urea, followed by heating at 700 °C for 2 h in N2 atmosphere [28]. As for Pd-based catalysts synthesized by the impregnation method, 1.5 g of support material (i.e., SBA-15, NCNTs, or NC-SBA-15) was immersed in 20 ml of distilled water with a certain amount of H2PdCl4. After stirring for 2 h, the suspension was dried at 80 °C in a water bath. The sample was reduced at 300 °C for 2 h in a 30 ml min−1 H2. The obtained catalysts were designated as im-Pd(x)/SBA-15, Pd(x)/NCNTs or im-Pd(x)/NC-SBA-15, where × referred to the Pd loading (wt.%) measured by ICP-OES. The catalysts were preserved in a desiccator filled with N2 before use.

The Pd loading amounts of catalysts were measured using ICP-OES (PerkinElmer 8300, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a powder diffraction-meter (Rigaku D/max-RA, Rigaku, Japan) in the range of 2θ from 0.6° to 10° and 10° to 80° with Cu Kα radiation for small angle and wide angle, respectively. The elemental distributions were conducted on an ARM-200F TEM (JEOL, Japan) equipped with an X-MAX energy dispersive spectrometer (Oxford, U.K.). The pore structures of the catalysts were characterized on a Micrometrics ASAP 2020 (Micromeritics Instrument Co., USA) apparatus at −196 °C (77 K) using the N2 adsorption method. Prior to N2 adsorption measurement, the catalysts were pretreated at 200 °C for 5 h under vacuum. The contents of C and N elements were obtained using a vario MICRO cube (Elementar, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of NCQDs and all of catalysts were performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The valence state of surface elements was detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a PHI5000 Versa Probe having a monochromatized Al Kα excitation source (hν = 1486.6 eV) (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). The binding energy values were calibrated with that of C 1 s peak (284.6 eV). Surface zeta potentials of mesoporous silica samples were measured in deionized water using a ZEN 3500 Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, U.K.). Prior to the measurement, the catalyst suspension (0.1 g l−1) was pre-adjusted to pH range of 2–10 and equilibrated for 24 h.

The aqueous phase catalytic reaction was performed at ambient conditions in a magnetically stirred 250 ml four-necked flask. Briefly, 15 mg of catalyst was introduced into 200 ml of aqueous solution containing 2,4-D with a desired initial concentration. Prior to reaction, the reactor was purged continuously by N2 (100 ml min−1) for 30 min under magnetically stirring. Then H2 (150 ml min−1) replaced N2 to pass through the reactor and catalytic reaction started. Liquid samples were withdrawn from the flask by fast filtration using 0.45 μm membrane at selected time intervals. The concentrations of 2,4-D, reaction intermediates and products in the filtrates were analyzed by an Agilent 1200 Series high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) equipped with an ultraviolet detector and a reversed phase C18 column (150 × 4.6 mm, 5 μm, Agilent). Elution was performed at 20 °C using an eluent of phosphoric acid (0.05%)/methanol (40:60, v:v, 1.0 ml min−1) with an emission wavelength of 226 nm.

Elemental analysis of NCQDs showed that NCQDs consisted of 71.6 wt% of C, 16.1 wt% of N, 3.1 wt% of H and 9.2 wt% of O, indicative of a very high N-doping level and surface functionality. For all supported Pd catalysts, Pd loading amounts varied within 1.4 wt%–1.7 wt%, reflecting almost identical Pd loading amounts in the catalysts. The small-angle XRD patterns of SBA-15 and supported Pd catalysts are shown in Fig. S1. SBA-15 had three distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ = 0.84°, 1.56° and 1.80°, respectively corresponding to (100), (110) and (200) planes, indicating that SBA-15 was associated with p6mm hexagonal symmetry [29,30]. In parallel, other SBA-15 supported Pd catalysts exhibited clear diffraction peaks of (100), (110) and (200), indicative of the high stability of SBA-15 support during Pd and/or NCQDs-loading process. Fig. 1 presents the wide-angle XRD patterns of SBA-15 and Pd-based catalysts. A broad diffraction peak with 2θ around 26° was observed in all SBA-15 supported samples, characteristic of amorphous SiO2 [31]. As for Pd(1.5)/NCNTs, two broad peaks with 2θ at 26° and 44° were observed, attributed to (002) and (101) diffraction peaks from crystalline graphitic carbon [32]. Notably, im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15 had three new diffraction peaks around 39.4°, 45.8° and 66.8° respectively indexed as (111), (200) and (220) planes of metallic Pd with a face-centered cubic structure [33,34], revealing that Pd nanoparticles were successfully deposited on support surface. However, similar diffraction peaks were not clearly identified on Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15, implying its higher Pd dispersion and smaller Pd particle size than those of im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, however, the intensity of the diffraction peaks from metallic Pd in Pd-NC@SBA-15 differed with NCQD loadings with the minimum peak intensity at a NCQD loading of 2 ml, while further increase of NCQD loading led to increased intensity of the diffraction peaks. The strong correlation between Pd dispersion and NCQD loading can be attributed to the bonding of Pd nanoparticles with NCQDs with varied dispersions [35].

To investigate Pd distributions in Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15, EDS elemental mapping was conducted and the images of N and Pd elemental mapping are compiled in Fig. S2. The presence of N and Pd in Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 indicated that the NCQDs and Pd were successfully loaded on the support. The excellent overlapping of N elements and Pd elements indicated that Pd on Pd-NC@SBA-15 was close contacted with NCQDs.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and the pore size distributions of SBA-15 and Pd-based catalysts are shown in Fig. S3, and the calculated structure parameters are listed in Table 1 . Except Pd(1.5)/NCNTs, type IV isotherms with H1 hysteresis loop were observed on all SBA-15 supported catalysts, characteristic of their typical mesoporous nature [36]. The introduction of Pd and/or NCQDs resulted in decreased Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area (S BET) and total pore volume (V t), suggesting that Pd and/or NCQDs were completely or partially loaded in SBA-15 pores. Notably, despite of similar loading amount of Pd and NCQDs shown in Table 2 , the S BET and V t of im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15 were lower than those of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15, likely due to pore blocking from NCQDs impregnation. The most probable pore size of im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15 was 14.8 nm (Fig. S3b and Table 1), similar to that of SBA-15, while Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 displayed a smaller pore size with a most probable pore size of 12.7 nm, suggesting effective confinement of Pd and NCQDs in SBA-15 pores. Additionally, the S BET and V t of the Pd-NC@SBA-15 catalysts substantially decreased with NCQD loading amount, confirming the confinement of Pd and NCQDs in the pore of SBA-15.

TEM images and histograms of NCQDs and supported Pd catalysts are displayed in Fig. 2 . NCQDs were found to be spherical in shape with a narrow size distribution in the range of 1.5–5 nm, which was accessible to the pores of SBA-15. In comparison with im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15, much smaller Pd particles and narrower particle size distribution were observed on Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15. The average Pd particle sizes of the catalysts were estimated based on surface area weighted diameter [6]:

where n i is the number of selected Pd particles with a diameter of d i. The total counting number (∑n i) of Pd particles is larger than 300.

For Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15, even Pd particle distribution was obtained within 0–5.0 nm and the average Pd particles size was calculated to be 2.32 nm. However, particle size distributions of im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15 ranged from 0 to 14.0 nm, 0 to 10.9 nm and 0 to 11.0 nm, respectively. Accordingly, the average Pd particle sizes were 6.75, 5.09 and 4.91 nm for im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15, respectively. The results clearly indicated that the formation of Pd-NCQD composite led to more even Pd particle distribution and smaller Pd particles. As indicated by XPS analysis, N species in NCQDs mainly consisted of pyridine N that can act as anchoring sites for Pd particles, resulting in high Pd distribution [13]. Furthermore, Pd particle sizes and dispersion on the Pd-NC@SBA-15 catalysts were found to be dependent on NCQD content in Pd-NCQDs composite. The average Pd particle sizes of Pd(1.4)-NC(1)@SBA-15, Pd(1.7)-NC(1.5)@SBA-15, Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15, Pd(1.5)-NC(3)@SBA-15 and Pd(1.6)-NC(4)@SBA-15 were calculated to be 5.40, 3.50, 2.32, 3.69 and 4.38 nm, respectively, indicative of an inverted volcano-type dependency of Pd particles size on NCQD loading amount. At NCQD loading below 2 ml, increasing NCQD loading resulted in decreased Pd particle size and enhanced Pd dispersion because of more anchoring sites from NCQDs available for Pd particles. At NCQD loading at 3 ml, however, remarkable Pd particle growth was identified as compared with Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15, likely because of the aggregation of NCQDs in SBA-15 pores resulting from the high NCQD loading. In parallel, further Pd particle growth with NCQD loading was also observed on Pd(1.6)-NC(4)@SBA-15, reflecting that aggregation of NCQDs at high loadings in SBA-15 pores resulted in Pd particle growth.

The compositions and electron valence states of surface elements in the catalysts were obtained using XPS. Except Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and Pd(1.6)-NC(4)@SBA-15 with the highest NCQD loading amount, other Pd-based catalysts did not give clear and informative N 1s spectra likely owing to low NCQD loading amounts and confinement of NCQDs in the pores. The XPS spectrum of N1s of Pd(1.6)-NC(4)@SBA-15 is showed in Fig. S5. An asymmetric broad peak ranging from 396.0 to 405.0 eV was observed in the N1s region, which was deconvoluted into three components. The peaks at 398.2, 399.5 and 401.0 eV were indexed as pyridinic (N1), pyrrolic (N2) and quaternary (N3) N species, respectively [37]. The ratio of N1/(N1 + N2 + N3) was calculated to be 50.4%, indicating that pyridinic N was the major N species in NCQDs. Similar XPS spectrum was obtained on Pd(1.5)/NCNTs in the N 1s region, whereas the N1/(N1 + N2 + N3) ratio of Pd(1.5)/NCNTs was 20%, much lower than that of Pd-NC@SBA-15.

The Pd 3d XPS spectra of the catalysts are shown in Fig. 3 . The spectra were composed of two asymmetric peaks indexed as Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2, which could be deconvoluted into two pairs of doublets. The peaks around 335.3 and 340.3 eV were referred to metallic Pd species and the peaks around 336.5 and 341.8 eV were corresponding to Pdn+ species. The presence of Pdn+ was ascribed to electron transfer from Pd metal to support because of strong metal-support interaction [12]. The Pdn+/Pd0 ratio of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 was calculated to be 0.85, which was markedly higher than those of im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15, indicative of a stronger metal-support interaction on Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15. The enhanced metal-support interaction on Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 could be ascribed to the presence of pyridinic N in NCQDs. Previous research [13,38] showed the presence of Lewis basic sites, such as pyridinic N species, in N-doped carbon easily induced the formation of positively charged Pdn+ species due to the strong binding of Pd particles on surface pyridinic N atoms. Despite of similar NCQD loading amounts, the stronger metal-support interaction in Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 than im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15 could be ascribed to the intimate contact and bonding of Pd particles with NCQDs due to the formation of Pd-NCQD composites in SBA-15 pores. In parallel, Pdn+/Pd0 ratio of Pd(1.5)/NCNTs was 0.38, much lower than that of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 due to very lower N-doping level and pyridinic N content of NCNTs. Additionally, Pd particles were randomly deposited on NCNTs surface, leading to low utilization of pyridinic N functionality. Accordingly, the Pdn+/Pd0 ratio of the Pd-NC@SBA-15 catalysts increased from 0.40 to 1.42 with NCQD loading amount from 1 to 4 ml, confirming effective formation of Pd-NCQD composites in SBA-15 and strong metal-support interaction between NCQDs and Pd particles. Notably, the surface Pd atomic concentration of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 was 0.88 at.%, much lower than that of im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15 (1.30 at.%) and Pd(1.5)/NCNTs (1.78 at.%), implying that Pd particles of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 were mainly loaded in the pores of SBA-15.

Preliminary experiments showed that 2,4-D adsorption equilibrium on catalysts was achieved within 30 min and the removal of 2,4-D due to catalyst adsorption was below 1% under our experimental conditions. For catalytic HDC of 2,4-D on Pd(1.5)-N(2)@SBA-15 (resulted shown in Fig. S6), 2-chlorophenoxyacetic acid (2-CPA) and 4-chlorophenoxyacetic acid (4-CPA) were detected as the intermediates and phenoxyacetic acid (PA) was detected as the final product. During the catalytic hydrodechlorination process, a higher 2-CPA concentration was detected than that of 4-CPA due to prohibited formation of 4-CPA from the steric hindrance effect [39]. Prior to the test of activities of the catalysts, the potential mass transfer limitation was evaluated. The catalytic HDC of 2,4-D over Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 was conducted using catalyst dosages of 0.05, 0.075 and 0.10 g l−1 and the results are compared in Fig. S7. The catalytic activity increased with the increase of catalyst dosage. However, when normalized by catalyst dosage, the initial activity kept constant, reflecting that the effect of mass transfer limitation could be negligible during the reaction process [40].

For a heterogeneous catalytic reaction, the reaction activity could be influenced by reactant adsorption on catalyst surface [41]. Hence, the effect of 2,4-D adsorption on catalytic activity was evaluated on Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 by varying the initial concentration of 2,4-D. The catalytic activities with various 2,4-D initial concentrations are shown in Fig. S8 and the dependency of initial activity on the initial concentration is shown in Fig. 4 a. When 2,4-D initial concentration varied from 0.10 to 0.75 mM, the initial conversion increased from 0.15 to 0.40 mM gCat−1 min−1, displaying a positive impact of 2,4-D adsorption on catalytic conversion. The relationship between reaction rate and initial concentration was simulated using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood equation [42]:

where r 0 is the initial activity of 2,4-D HDC at C 0, θ s is the coverage of 2,4-D on the surface of catalyst, and b and k are the equilibrium constant and reaction rate constant, respectively.

Simulation results are displayed in Fig. 4b. Good correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.95) was observed on the plot of 1/C 0 versus 1/r 0, indicating that HDC of 2,4-D over Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 well fitted the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model. This result also demonstrated that the dechlorination of 2,4-D was controlled by the adsorption of 2,4-D on catalyst surface.

The catalysts with different supports exhibited different catalytic activities as shown in Fig. 5 . For Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15, 100% conversion of 2,4-D was achieved within 100 min, while only 12%, 38% and 78% of 2,4-D were removed respectively on im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15, reflecting the higher activity of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 than those of im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15. Because the catalysts had similar Pd loadings, the significant difference in catalytic activity was related to their structural properties. TEM analysis showed that the presence of NCQDs with close contact with Pd facilitated dispersion of Pd particles, leading to smaller Pd particles and higher content of Pd active sites than those of im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15 [43–45]. As a result, Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 displayed a higher catalytic activity. To further clarify the catalytic mechanism, the turnover frequency (TOF) of Pd in the supported catalysts was calculated. The TOF values were determined by normalizing the initial rate constants with exposed Pd sites, in which the exposed Pd site was estimated based on TEM results. The TOF values were calculated to be 0.056, 0.093, 0.30 and 0.45 s−1 for im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs, im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15 and Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15, respectively, confirming the higher catalytic activity of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15. Previous studies proposed that the presence of both Pd0 and Pdn+ sites in Pd based catalysts was essential for the liquid phase catalytic HDC because Pd0 served as active sites for the dissociation of H2 and Pdn+ for the activation of C–X (X = Cl, Br and I) bond [46,47]. The XPS results showed that the three catalysts had both Pd0 and Pdn+ species, whereas the Pdn+/Pd0 ratio of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 was 0.85, much higher compared with those of im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, Pd(1.5)/NCNTs and im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15. Accordingly, the higher TOF value observed on the catalyst with a higher Pdn+/Pd0 ratio indicated that the activation and clearance of C-Cl bond was a more crucial step than H2 dissociation in the catalytic conversion of 2,4-D. Similar results were observed previously [48]. Additionally, the formation of Pd-NCQD composites resulted in a higher Pdn+/Pd0 ratio in Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15, contributing to the higher activity of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15.

The impact of NCQD loading amount on Pd(1.5)-NC@SBA-15 activity is shown in Fig. 6 . Increasing NCQD amount from 1 to 3 ml resulted in increased initial activity of the catalyst from 0.078 to 0.27 mM gCat−1 min−1, while further increasing NCQD amount led to gradually decreased initial activity, displaying a volcano-type dependency of catalytic activity on NCQD loading. Considering that the catalysts had similar Pd loading amounts, the different activities were strongly linked with the structural properties of Pd particles. As aforementioned, an inverted volcano-type relationship was obtained between average Pd particle size and NCQD loading amount with the smallest particle size identified in Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15. The strong dependency of catalytic activity on NCQD amount suggested that the catalytic activity of the Pd-NC@SBA-15 catalyst was closely related to its average Pd particle size. For example, Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 had the highest catalytic activity due to its smallest Pd particle size among the catalysts. In principle, given an identical Pd loading amount, the catalyst with smaller Pd particle size presented higher Pd dispersion and more active Pd sites, displaying an enhanced catalytic activity. Notably, both Pd(1.5)-NC(3)@SBA-15 and Pd(1.6)-NC(4)@SBA-15 were more active than Pd(1.7)-NC(1.5)@SBA-15 although Pd(1.7)-NC(1.5)@SBA-15 had a smaller Pd particle size (see Table 3 ), implying that other structural properties of Pd particles also impacted the catalyst activity. To further verify the inherent mechanism, the TOF values of the catalysts were calculated and the dependency of TOF values on the ratio of Pdn+/Pd0 is presented in Fig. 7 . Interestingly, the TOF value of the catalyst increased with Pdn+/Pd0 value, reflecting a positive relationship between the catalytic activity and Pdn+/Pd0 value of active Pd sites of the catalysts. It had been previously concluded that for liquid phase HDC of chlorinated pollutants the activation of H2 and C-Cl bonds was implemented on metallic and positively charged Pd sites, respectively. The higher catalytic activity of the catalyst with a higher Pdn+/Pd0 value further confirmed the decisive role of C-Cl activation in catalytic HDC of 2,4-D. Additionally, TOF values of Pd site of the catalysts gently increased with Pdn+/Pd0 at Pdn+/Pd0 value around 1.27, implying a maximized catalytic activity of active Pd site. Similar results were also observed previously [49,50]. To compare the catalytic activities of the catalysts, some results were obtained from literatures and listed in Table S1. The TOF values of the catalysts were found to be higher than those previously reported in catalytic HDC of 2,4-D [51], highlighting the high catalytic activity of Pd-NCQD@SBA-15.

To test the reusability of the catalyst, Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 was reused in three catalyst cycles and the results are shown in Fig. S9. During catalyst recycle, the catalytic activity gradually decreased, likely because of gradual poison of active Pd particles by chlorine and leaching of Pd particles [43,52].

Because 2,4-D is a polychlorinated compound, catalytic HDC of 2,4-D to PA could be accomplished via two different pathways, i.e., stepwise and concerted pathways (Scheme 1 ) [53,54]. Hence, the pathway of dechlorination of 2,4-D was verified by fitting the experimental data to kinetic equations [5]. Given that the catalytic HDC reaction follows pseudo-first order kinetics and H2 concentration in solution is constant due to excess hydrogen under the reaction conditions (150 ml min−1 of H2 flow rate), the concentrations of 2,4-D, 2-CPA, 4-CPA and PA as a function of reaction time can be obtained as follows.

The experimental data were fitted to Eqs. (8)–(11) and the resulting rate constants are compared in Table 4 . All rate constants from data fitting were positive, indicating that 2,4-D was dechlorinated via both concerted and stepwise pathways on all tested catalysts. The rate constants of 2,4-D HDC (k 1 + k 2 + k 3) over im-Pd(1.6)/SBA-15, im-Pd(1.5)/NC-SBA-15 and Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 were 0.0012, 0.012 and 0.043 min−1, respectively, confirming the highest catalytic activity of Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15. Additionally, the rate constant of k 2 were higher than k 1 for all catalysts, further confirming that the Cl atom at para-position was more vulnerable to be removed than ortho-position Cl because of steric hindrance effect. Furthermore, the (k 1 + k 2)/k 3 ratios of all catalysts were higher than 1, indicating that 2,4-D was dechlorinated predominantly via the stepwise pathway.

In the present study, Pd-NCQD composites confined in SBA-15 were prepared and their catalytic performances for the aqueous phase catalytic HDC of 2,4-D were investigated. For comparison, Pd/NCNTs, im-Pd/SBA-15 and im-Pd/NC-SBA-15 with similar Pd loadings were prepared by the impregnation method. Compared with im-Pd/SBA-15, Pd/NCNTs and im-Pd/NC-SBA-15, Pd-NC@SBA-15 has a higher Pd dispersion and more homogeneous Pd distribution due to effective anchoring of Pd particles on N-containing functionalities from NCQDs resulting from the strong interaction between Pd particles and NCQDs. In addition, the correlation between NCQD loading and Pd particle size followed an inverted volcano-type relationship and Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 has the smallest Pd particles size and the highest Pd dispersion among the tested catalysts. The strong interaction between Pd and NCQDs also leads to increased content of Pdn+ and the ratio of Pdn+/Pd0 in the catalysts increases with NCQD content. In the catalytic HDC of 2,4-D, Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 exhibits a much higher catalytic activity than those of im-Pd/SBA-15, Pd/NCNTs and im-Pd/NC-SBA-15. Accordingly, the highest TOF value is also observed on Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 due to its highest content of Pdn+ among the tested catalysts. The catalytic HDC of 2,4-D on Pd(1.5)-NC(2)@SBA-15 follows the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model and the reaction is controlled by the conversion of 2,4-D adsorbed on the surface of catalysts. The HDC of 2,4-D proceeds through both stepwise and concerted pathways and the stepwise dechlorination pathway was predominant. The present study highlights that substantial enhancement of the catalytic activities can be achieved by NCQD modification of Pd based catalysts for the aqueous phase catalytic HDC of pollutants in water.
